title: GDP per capita
subtitle: This data is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.
caption:
GDP per capita, 2020
This data is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.

The choropleth map displays the distribution of GDP per capita across the globe for the year 2020, with values expressed in international dollars at 1990 prices to account for inflation and cost-of-living differences. The visualization utilizes a continuous color scale ranging from light beige for the lowest income bracket of $0 to $1,000, transitioning through shades of green and teal, up to dark blue for the highest bracket exceeding $20,000. The highest values, represented by the darkest blue, are concentrated in North America, specifically the United States and Canada, as well as in Western Europe including Norway, Switzerland, and Ireland, alongside Australia and Japan. A secondary tier of high income, falling within the $10,000 to $20,000 range shown in medium blue, covers much of the European Union, including France, Germany, and the United Kingdom, as well as South Korea and New Zealand. In contrast, the lowest income levels, depicted in pale beige and light green corresponding to the $0 to $2,000 range, dominate the African continent, with countries such as Niger, Chad, and the Democratic Republic of the Congo falling into the bottom category. Large portions of South Asia, including India and Pakistan, along with parts of Southeast Asia and Central America, occupy the lower-middle bands between $2,000 and $5,000. South America and Eastern Europe generally appear in the middle bands, ranging from $5,000 to $10,000. Regions such as Greenland and parts of the Middle East are marked with diagonal hatching to indicate that no data is available. The geographic pattern shows a clear concentration of the highest economic output per person in the Northern Hemisphere's developed economies, while the lowest values cluster across sub-Saharan Africa and parts of South Asia.
